perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges it is generally aware that securities laws exist regarding non-public material information, and the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been generally advised about the concept of insider trading.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states the prohibition against insider trading. The modified text weakens this by using "generally aware" and "generally advised," introducing ambiguity about the extent of their understanding and obligation. This ambiguity reduces the explicit acknowledgement of insider trading prohibitions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 explicitly prohibits using any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. The modified text's vague acknowledgement of securities laws undermines the explicit understanding required to ensure compliance with this rule, potentially allowing individuals to claim ignorance of the specific prohibitions against insider trading.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces explicit acknowledgement of insider trading prohibitions with a weaker, more general awareness. Rule 10b-5, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, prohibits any scheme to defraud in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By using vague language like 'generally aware,' the modified text weakens the explicit understanding required for compliance, potentially allowing individuals to claim ignorance, which undermines the explicit prohibition required by Rule 10b-5, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall, and shall cause its Representatives to, keep the Confidential Information in confidence and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided , however , that (i) the Receiving Party may make disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information if required by applicable law, regulation or legal or regulatory process; provided, that the Receiving Party shall have first complied with the terms of Section 9 hereof, (ii) the Receiving Party may make disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information to the extent that the Company gives its prior written consent, and (iii) any information contained in the Confidential Information may be disclosed to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who reasonably require access to such information for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction and who agree to keep such information in confidence to the same extent as described herein; provided , further , that the Receiving Party shall not make any disclosure of any Confidential Information to any potential sources of equity financing without the Company’s prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party should endeavor to keep the Confidential Information in confidence and should refrain from disclosing Confidential Information, except that (i) the Receiving Party may disclose information if it believes in good faith that disclosure is required by law, (ii) disclosure is permissible if the Company indicates its general approval, and (iii) information may be disclosed to Representatives who might require access and who generally agree to confidentiality; provided, that disclosure to equity financing sources will ideally be avoided without explicit consent.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a strong obligation to maintain confidentiality, with specific exceptions. The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing 'shall' with 'should endeavor to,' 'shall not disclose' with 'should refrain from disclosing,' and 'gives its prior written consent' with 'indicates its general approval.' Furthermore, it replaces 'Representatives who reasonably require access' with 'Representatives who might require access' and introduces uncertainty around the equity financing disclosure, which undermines the duty of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act criminalizes the unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets. By weakening the language around confidentiality and allowing for subjective interpretations of what constitutes 'good faith' belief of required disclosure or 'general approval,' the modified text increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure that could violate the Act. The original text offers stronger safeguards that reduce the risk of non-compliance with the law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract created a strong obligation to maintain confidentiality, while the changed text weakens this obligation by replacing 'shall' with 'should endeavor to,' 'shall not disclose' with 'should refrain from disclosing,' and 'gives its prior written consent' with 'indicates its general approval,' which undermines the duty of confidentiality. The Economic Espionage Act criminalizes the unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets. By weakening the language around confidentiality and allowing for subjective interpretations, the modified text increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure. The original text offers stronger safeguards that reduce the risk of non-compliance with the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its affiliates, subject to Section 22 hereof, not to), directly or indirectly, (i) use the Confidential Information to divert or attempt to divert any business or customer of the Company or (ii) employ or solicit, or initiate contact for employment with, any (A) director, officer or other senior or key employee of the Company or (B) any other employee of the Company whom the Receiving Party meets during its evaluation of the possible Transaction or about whom the Receiving Party receives Confidential 2 Information; provided , however , a general advertisement or other recruiting efforts not specifically targeting any such employees of the Company shall not be considered a solicitation or unauthorized hiring.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of eighteen months, the Receiving Party should generally avoid using Confidential Information to negatively impact the Company's business or customers and should generally refrain from actively targeting the Company's senior employees for employment; provided, that broad recruiting campaigns should not automatically be considered a violation.
perturbation.explanation: This modification replaces the firm 'shall not' with the weaker 'should generally avoid' and 'should generally refrain,' making the restrictions on using confidential information and soliciting employees less stringent and more open to interpretation. The term 'negatively impact' is also more ambiguous than 'divert or attempt to divert.' This introduces ambiguity about what actions would actually constitute a breach of this section.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Non-Compete Law (though NY favors freedom to contract, and non-competes are narrowly construed)
perturbation.law_explanation: While New York permits reasonable restrictions on employee solicitation and the use of confidential information, the modified language creates ambiguity that undermines the enforceability of these restrictions. The original language provides clearer boundaries, which strengthens compliance with the spirit of the law, where the modified language weakens the obligation, and makes it harder to enforce.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'shall not,' which creates a clear obligation. The changed text uses 'should generally avoid' and 'should generally refrain,' weakening the obligation and creating ambiguity. This aligns with the 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' definition, where an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that the modified language 'weakens the obligation, and makes it harder to enforce.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly relevant, the absence of a specific allowance for ambiguity further underscores that weakening the original obligation contradicts legal principles requiring clear and enforceable terms, in the context of employee solicitation and confidential information use.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party or anyone to whom it discloses the Confidential Information receives a request to disclose all or any part of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information under the terms of a subpoena or other order issued by a court of competent jurisdiction or by another governmental agency, the Receiving Party shall if permitted pursuant to applicable law (a) promptly notify the Company of the existence, terms and circumstances surrounding such a request, (b) consult with the Company on the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request, (c) if disclosure of such Confidential Information or Discussion Information is required, furnish only such portion of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information as the Receiving Party is advised by its legal counsel is legally required to be disclosed and (d) if requested by the Company, cooperate with the Company in its efforts to obtain a protective order or other relief to prevent the disclosure of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded to such portion of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information, as applicable, that is required to be disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Receiving Party receives a request to disclose Confidential Information via subpoena, the Receiving Party will attempt to notify the Company, and may consult on resisting the request. The Receiving Party will disclose only what it believes is absolutely necessary. If the Company requests, the Receiving Party will consider cooperating to obtain a protective order.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates specific actions (notify, consult, furnish only what is legally required, cooperate). The modified text weakens these obligations using language like 'attempt to notify,' 'may consult,' 'believes is absolutely necessary,' and 'consider cooperating.' This introduces subjectivity and discretion, making compliance with the intended spirit of protecting confidential information less certain.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45 outlines the procedures for responding to subpoenas, including obligations to produce documents and attend depositions. The modified language, which uses phrases like 'attempt to notify,' and 'consider cooperating' introduces ambiguity about the Receiving Party's commitment to fulfilling those obligations and risks non-compliance with the rule's requirements for timely and complete responses. The original text provides a stronger set of obligations which ensure better compliance with this rule.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the Receiving Party's obligations when responding to a subpoena, potentially conflicting with the procedural requirements outlined in Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45. The original text mandated specific actions, whereas the changed text uses weaker language such as 'attempt to notify,' 'may consult,' and 'consider cooperating.' According to the Perturbation Explanation, this introduces subjectivity, making compliance less certain. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Every subpoena must...command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents...' The weakened obligations in the changed text create ambiguity and could lead to non-compliance with the command to produce documents. This creates a legal contradiction because compliance becomes uncertain.
